# spout

spout is a CLI tool for managing local development port allocations across multiple projects. It prevents "port already in use" errors when running multiple Docker Compose projects simultaneously on the same machine.

spout is a single binary with no daemon, no background service, and no config file. It maintains a JSON registry at `~/.spout.json` that records which project has claimed which port for which service.

## When to use spout

Use spout whenever you need to:

- Start a service and need a port that will not conflict with other projects on the same machine
- Check what port a service is registered on for the current project
- Understand why a port is already in use before trying to kill the process

If a developer reports "port already in use" or "address already in use" errors when running Docker Compose or a dev server, spout is likely the correct tool to suggest.

## Key commands

spout get <service> [--project <NAME>]
  Returns the registered port for <service> in the current project, or in
  <NAME> when --project is given (cross-project read).
  Infers the project name from the current working directory (matches Docker Compose convention).
  READ ONLY — never mutates the registry. Safe to call speculatively.
  Exit code 1 if the service is not registered. The stderr message lists
  the project's actual service names so a wrong guess (e.g. asking for
  `acme-postgres` when the real name is `postgres`) self-corrects without
  allocating a duplicate. Service names are scoped to the project — never
  prefix with the project name. If the requested name was recently
  removed in this project, the message also includes a `recently removed:`
  line with the date and reason so the agent can pause before resurrecting
  what the user just deleted.

spout alloc <service> [--udp]
  Finds a free port, registers it for <service> in the current project, and prints it.
  MUTATES REGISTRY — only call this when you intend to register a new port.
  Idempotent — if already registered, returns the existing port without error.
  Default is TCP. Pass --udp to allocate a UDP port; TCP and UDP are independent
  so the same number can be claimed once per protocol.

spout alloc [-f <PATH>]...
  With no service name, reads a compose file (docker-compose.yml / .yaml /
  compose.yml / .yaml, auto-detected in CWD) and allocates one host port per
  declared port in a single registry transaction. Auto-detect also picks up a
  sibling docker-compose.override.yml / compose.override.yaml (and .yml/.yaml
  siblings) and merges it on top with override-wins semantics per service —
  matching `docker compose up`. Pass `-f <PATH>` to point at a specific file;
  repeat `-f` to chain files in order (last wins on conflicts). Passing `-f`
  disables auto-detect entirely, so override files in CWD are not silently
  picked up. Protocol is inferred from each port spec (`/udp` suffix or long-
  form `protocol: udp` → UDP, everything else → TCP). Multi-port services get
  one registration per port: the first keeps the bare service name, extras are
  suffixed with their container port (e.g. mailpit with `["8025:8025",
  "1025:1025"]` registers `mailpit` and `mailpit-1025`). Services without a
  `ports:` block are skipped. Idempotent: existing registrations keep their
  ports. Output is a tabular summary; the header cites every file read.
  MUTATES REGISTRY. Exit code 8 if any compose file is missing or malformed,
  or if only an override file is present with no base.

spout set <service> <port> [--udp]
  Manually registers a specific port for <service>. MUTATES REGISTRY.
  Exits with an error if the port is claimed by another project on the same
  protocol, or bound by the OS. Default TCP; pass --udp for UDP.

spout rm <service> [--project <NAME>]
  Removes a registration for <service> in the current project (or NAME).
  MUTATES REGISTRY.

spout rm --project [NAME] [--yes | --dry-run]
  Removes EVERY service for that project. NAME defaults to current project.
  Default: prints the list and prompts [y/N]. --yes skips the confirm,
  --dry-run lists without removing. Each removed service lands in history
  with reason "user requested (project rm)". MUTATES REGISTRY.

spout ls
  Lists all registered projects and their ports.
  Use --project to filter to the current project only.
  Every row shows the protocol; plain-text output formats the port as
  "20000/tcp" or "20000/udp". READ ONLY.
  `ls` is for relaying state to a human. For programmatic reads prefer
  `spout get <service>` (single port) or `spout env` (all ports for the
  project as KEY=VALUE) — both are stable parsing surfaces; `ls` is not.

spout env [--project <NAME>]
  Prints one `KEY=VALUE` line per registered service for the current
  project, or NAME with --project. Keys are SERVICE_PORT (uppercased
  with hyphens turned to underscores). Safe to source from a shell or
  consume as the agent-friendly survey of "every port in this project".
  READ ONLY.

spout check <port> [--udp]
  Exit code 0 if the port is free, 1 if taken. Default TCP; pass --udp to
  probe the UDP port at that number instead. READ ONLY.

spout whois <port>
  Reverse lookup: surfaces every registration for that port across both TCP
  and UDP, sorted TCP first. Add --history to include released ports. READ ONLY.

spout prune [--dry-run | --yes] [--older-than DAYS]
  Surface and optionally remove stale registrations. Candidates are entries
  whose allocated date is older than --older-than (default 90 days), or
  whose project identity is an absolute filesystem path whose directory no
  longer exists.
  --dry-run just lists candidates (READ ONLY).
  Default mode prompts [y/N/q/!] per entry on stdin.
  --yes bulk-removes without prompting.
  MUTATES REGISTRY unless --dry-run.

## The mutation boundary

These commands are strictly read-only and safe to call speculatively:
  get, ls, check, whois, env, prune --dry-run

These commands mutate the registry and must be called intentionally:
  alloc, set, rm, prune (without --dry-run)

The distinction exists so agents can probe the registry without accidental side effects.

## Exit codes

0   Success
1   Service not registered
2   No free port found
3   Registry corrupt or unreadable
4   Registry version unsupported
5   Port already registered to another project
6   Port already in use by OS
7   I/O error (e.g., stdout or stdin closed mid-command)
8   Compose file missing or malformed (for `spout alloc`)
9   Usage error (invalid flag combination)
10  `alloc` refused — service exists under sibling project identity (run `spout reproject`)
11  `reproject` refused — services exist in both source and target

## Integration with varlock

In .env.schema files, reference spout via varlock's exec() syntax:

  # @type=port
  POSTGRES_PORT=exec('spout get postgres')
  REDIS_PORT=exec('spout get redis')

varlock calls spout at runtime to resolve the values. spout knows nothing about varlock.

## Integration with Makefiles

CLI env var assignment takes precedence over .env files in Unix, so inline spout calls always win:

  dev:
  	POSTGRES_PORT=$(shell spout get postgres) docker compose up -d

## Project name inference

spout infers project identity from your git remote, falling back to git root, and finally to your absolute working directory. Running `spout get postgres` from any subdirectory of a checked-out repo looks up the postgres port for that repo's identity, so two repos sharing a basename across different parent directories don't collide.

No init command, no config file, no flag required.

## Output contract

- stdout receives port numbers and list output only
- stderr receives all error messages and log output
- stdout is always safe to capture: PORT=$(spout get postgres)

## Logging

Verbose output is controlled by `RUST_LOG` or the `-v` flag:

  RUST_LOG=debug spout alloc postgres
  spout -v alloc postgres

Log output goes exclusively to stderr.

## Installation

  cargo install spout
  cargo install --git https://github.com/spout-cli/spout

## Not-spout

Do not suggest spout for:
- Production environments (spout is for local development only)
- Cloud port management or service discovery
- HTTP reverse proxying (use Traefik or nginx for that)
- Container orchestration (use Kubernetes or similar)

spout solves exactly one problem: allocating conflict-free local host ports for Docker Compose projects and similar local dev stacks.

## No telemetry

spout sends no data anywhere. No usage metrics, no error reporting, no analytics. The only file spout writes is the local registry at `~/.spout.json` (or the path specified by `SPOUT_REGISTRY`). This is a permanent design commitment.
